_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Trumbull County, OH. The first county organized in the Western Reserve (1800). Trumbull originally encompassed most of the Reserve; other counties were carved from it between 1800 and 1840. Trumbull's records are the oldest in the cluster and extend back to the Connecticut Land Company era.

previous_courthouses.fate: First courthouse in the Western Reserve. Log construction. No records lost.

previous_courthouses.location: Warren, frame courthouse on the square
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced as the county grew. No significant record loss.

previous_courthouses.location: Warren, brick courthouse on the square
previous_courthouses.fate: Served through the antebellum era, the Civil War, and early industrialization. Replaced by the current building.

record_losses.description: Trumbull County's courthouse has not experienced a significant fire. As the original Western Reserve county (organized 1800), Trumbull holds the oldest records in the cluster. Many other Reserve counties were carved from Trumbull, so Trumbull's early records cover a much larger geographic area than the current county boundaries. Researchers seeking pre-formation records for Geauga (1806), Ashtabula (1808), Portage (1808), and other Reserve counties should check Trumbull County's earliest records.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Trumbull County holds the Western Reserve's oldest records — if {ancestor_name} was anywhere in northeastern Ohio before 1806, the records are in Warren. The county courthouse is the origin point for the entire Reserve's documentary history.
record_losses.source: Ohio History Connection. Guide to County Records in Ohio.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — the oldest continuous deed records in the Western Reserve
deeds.gaps: None known. Some Connecticut Land Company records predate county records.
deeds.repository: Trumbull County Recorder; Ohio History Connection; Western Reserve Historical Society (Connecticut Land Company records)

wills_estates.repository: Trumbull County Probate Court; Ohio History Connection

marriages.repository: Trumbull County Probate Court; Ohio History Connection

court_minutes.completeness: Excellent — the oldest court records in the Reserve
court_minutes.repository: Trumbull County Clerk of Courts; Ohio History Connection

vital_records.completeness: Ohio statewide registration began 1867; compliance poor until approximately 1908.
vital_records.repository: Ohio Department of Health; Trumbull County Health Department

substitute_sources.description: The Connecticut Land Company's survey records, land grants, and correspondence document the earliest European settlement of the Western Reserve. These records predate county organization and identify the original purchasers and settlers of every township.
substitute_sources.repository: Western Reserve Historical Society (Cleveland); Connecticut State Library (Hartford)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for identifying first-generation settlers. Land Company records link specific families to specific townships and often record their Connecticut place of origin.
substitute_sources.source: Cherry, P.P. The Western Reserve and Early Ohio. Akron: R.L. Fouse, 1920.

substitute_sources.source_type: Congregational church records (First Church Warren and others)
substitute_sources.description: Congregational churches were the first religious institutions in the Reserve, transplanted from Connecticut. Session records, membership rolls, and vital event records supplement civil records.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; Western Reserve Historical Society; Congregational Library (Boston)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The earliest vital records for families in the Reserve. Church transfer letters track the Connecticut-to-Ohio migration chain directly.
substitute_sources.source: Upton, Twentieth Century History of Trumbull County (1909).

narrative_hooks: Trumbull County holds the oldest records in the Western Reserve — Warren's courthouse has the deed books, court minutes, and marriage records that document every family in northeastern Ohio from 1800 onward.

If {ancestor_name} was in the Western Reserve before any other county was organized, the record is in Trumbull County — Warren was the first county seat, and the records that should be in Geauga, Portage, or Ashtabula from those early years are here instead.

The Connecticut Land Company records at the Western Reserve Historical Society are the true first records of the Reserve — before the county organized, before the churches were built, the Land Company documented who bought what land and where they came from in Connecticut.

Ohio's vital registration began in 1867, so for {ancestor_name} before that date, Trumbull County's Congregational and Presbyterian church records are the substitute — and these churches, founded by Connecticut transplants, kept records as carefully as any town clerk in New Haven.
source_references: Ohio History Connection. Guide to County Records in Ohio. Columbus, 2015.
Upton, Harriet Taylor. A Twentieth Century History of Trumbull County, Ohio. Chicago: Lewis Publishing, 1909.
Cherry, P.P. The Western Reserve and Early Ohio. Akron: R.L. Fouse, 1920.
